policy.title: RBI Releases Final Climate Risk Management Guidelines for Scheduled Commercial Banks
policy.description: RBI mandates all scheduled commercial banks to integrate climate scenario analysis into ICAAP by March 2027, with quarterly physical risk reporting for top 100 exposures by value. Banks must establish board-level climate risk committees within 12 months.

policy.title: SEBI Expands BRSR Core to Top 500 Listed Companies with Assurance Mandate
policy.description: SEBI extended mandatory BRSR Core reporting with reasonable assurance to top 500 companies by market cap, effective FY2026-27. New requirements include Scope 3 Category 15 (investments) disclosure for financial sector entities.
policy.source: Securities and Exchange Board of India

policy.title: IRDAI Issues Draft Guidelines on Index-Based and Parametric Insurance Products
policy.description: IRDAI draft framework permits insurers to offer parametric products linked to weather indices, satellite data, and IoT sensors, with maximum payout triggers set at 72 hours post-event verification. Comments due by 30 May 2026.
policy.source: Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India

policy.title: Basel Committee Finalizes Climate-Related Financial Risk Disclosure Framework
policy.description: BCBS published final Pillar 3 climate disclosure requirements mandating standardized templates for transition risk exposures and physical risk concentration by geography. G-SIBs must comply by January 2027, other internationally active banks by 2028.

policy.title: ISSB Announces Jurisdictional Adoption Pathway for Emerging Markets
policy.description: IFRS Foundation released implementation guidance for ISSB S1/S2 adoption in emerging markets, with phased relief on Scope 3 and scenario analysis for companies below USD 500M revenue. India, Brazil, and South Africa confirmed adoption timelines for 2027.

policy.title: NGFS Releases Phase V Climate Scenarios with Enhanced Physical Risk Granularity
policy.description: Updated NGFS scenarios now include sub-national physical risk projections for 45 countries including state-level India data. New 'Climate Litigation' scenario added reflecting transition risks from legal liability exposure.
policy.source: Network for Greening the Financial System

competitors.title: Jupiter Intelligence Partners with HDFC Bank for India Physical Risk Assessment
competitors.description: Jupiter announced a multi-year engagement with HDFC Bank to provide climate risk scores for commercial real estate and infrastructure lending portfolios. The deal reportedly covers 15,000+ asset locations across India.

competitors.title: Sprih Raises $12M Series A to Expand Climate Risk Modules for Indian Banks
competitors.description: Bengaluru-based Sprih closed Series A led by Bertelsmann India Investments, with participation from existing investor Omnivore. Funds will accelerate development of physical risk analytics for NBFC lending.

competitors.title: MSCI Launches India Climate VaR Module with District-Level Hazard Mapping
competitors.description: MSCI ESG released India-specific Climate Value-at-Risk product covering 3,000+ listed companies with physical risk scores at district level. Pricing reportedly starts at $150K annually for institutional users.

competitors.title: XDI Partners with Yes Bank for Asset-Level Climate Risk Integration
competitors.description: Australian climate analytics firm XDI signed MoU with Yes Bank to embed physical risk scores into credit underwriting workflows. Pilot covers agricultural and SME lending in Maharashtra and Karnataka.

competitors.title: Watershed Raises $200M Series D at $3.5B Valuation, Eyes Asia Expansion
competitors.description: Watershed closed Series D led by Greenoaks Capital with participation from Kleiner Perkins. Company announced plans to open Singapore hub and build partnerships with APAC financial institutions by Q4 2026.

competitors.title: Moody's Integrates Physical Climate Risk into India Credit Ratings Methodology
competitors.description: Moody's announced physical climate risk will be explicitly incorporated into credit rating assessments for Indian corporates and sub-sovereigns from July 2026. Methodology covers flood, cyclone, and heat stress exposures.

research.title: Physical Climate Risk in Indian Bank Portfolios: Stress Testing Framework
research.description: RBI pilot study on 12 PSU banks found 8-14% of agricultural lending concentrated in districts with 'very high' flood or drought exposure. Report recommends PD adjustments of 50-200 bps for climate-vulnerable sectors.

research.title: Parametric Insurance for Climate Resilience: Lessons from South Asia
research.description: World Bank analysis of 23 parametric schemes across India, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka found basis risk averaging 18% in rainfall products but below 8% in satellite-verified flood products. Recommends hybrid trigger designs.

research.title: sigma 2/2026: Natural Catastrophe Losses in Asia-Pacific
research.description: Swiss Re estimates 2025 insured nat-cat losses in India at $8.2B, up 34% from 2024, driven by Chennai floods and Odisha cyclone. Protection gap in India remains above 90% for climate perils.

research.title: ESG Data Availability in Emerging Markets: 2026 Assessment
research.description: IFC assessment of 8,000 companies across 40 emerging markets found only 23% of Indian listed firms disclose Scope 1 emissions with third-party verification. Data quality scores lowest for biodiversity and water metrics.

research.title: Credit Risk Implications of Physical Climate Hazards
research.description: BIS working paper analyzing 50,000 corporate loans across 12 countries found flood exposure increases 1-year PD by 15-40 bps, with effects concentrated in firms with limited insurance coverage and fixed asset intensity above 60%.

research.title: Climate Risk Transfer Mechanisms for Agriculture in India
research.description: CRISIL study found parametric crop insurance could reduce claim settlement time from 120 days to under 15 days. Analysis of PMFBY data suggests 40% of delayed claims stem from yield assessment bottlenecks addressable by index triggers.

ourRead.headline: RBI's climate mandate creates a 12-month window where Indian banks will scramble for physical risk analytics—and global competitors are already at the door.
body: This month's signals converge on a single theme: the institutionalization of climate risk in Indian financial regulation is now irreversible, and the commercial battle for bank relationships is entering a decisive phase. RBI's final guidelines, SEBI's BRSR expansion, and IRDAI's parametric framework together create a regulatory stack that will force every major Indian FI to procure climate intelligence capabilities within the next 18 months.

The RBI's April 18 guidelines are the most consequential policy development for StepChange this quarter. The requirement for quarterly physical risk reporting on top 100 exposures by value, combined with ICAAP integration by March 2027, creates immediate procurement urgency at PSU and large private banks. The RBI's own pilot study (research-1) provides validation language StepChange should embed in sales materials—the 50-200 bps PD adjustment recommendation is exactly the output our models deliver. We should position our PD/LGD modules as RBI-methodology-aligned before competitors claim that ground.

The competitor signals are alarming. Jupiter's HDFC deal and XDI's Yes Bank partnership (comp-1, comp-4) indicate that global players are no longer treating India as a future market—they're executing now. Sprih's $12M raise (comp-2) shows domestic competitors are capitalizing too. However, the global players face localization challenges: Jupiter's 15,000 asset coverage sounds impressive but likely relies on coarse hazard layers. StepChange's district-level granularity and PMFBY-integrated datasets are defensible advantages, but only if we can demonstrate them in active pilots before these competitors entrench.

The World Bank's parametric insurance study (research-2) is directly actionable. The finding that satellite-verified flood products achieve sub-8% basis risk versus 18% for rainfall products should inform our product design conversations with banks exploring parametric structures under IRDAI's new framework. We should prioritize flood and cyclone triggers over rainfall in our parametric product partnerships—the research validates this will reduce basis risk complaints that have historically killed parametric adoption in India.

One thing to watch: Moody's integration of physical climate risk into India credit ratings (comp-6) from July 2026 will create a second-order demand surge as rated corporates seek to understand and contest their exposure scores. One action to take: accelerate outreach to the 12 PSU banks in RBI's pilot study—they now have board-level visibility on climate risk gaps and will be fastest to procure solutions.
